title_en: The Muhajirun: Companions Who Emigrated from Mecca
content_en:
The Muhajirun — those Companions who emigrated from Mecca to Medina — hold a place of special honor in the Quran and in Islamic tradition. Their emigration was not merely a geographical relocation but a profound act of faith: they left their homes, their property, their extended families, and their tribal networks for the sake of Allah and His messenger. The Quran praises them specifically: 'Those who believed and emigrated and strove with their wealth and their lives in the cause of Allah, and those who gave shelter and aid — they are allies of one another' (8:72). Ibn 'Abd al-Barr's entries on the Muhajirun capture both the historical details of their emigration and the spiritual significance that Islamic tradition has assigned to it.

The emigration to Medina in 1 AH (622 CE) was preceded by the earlier emigration to Abyssinia, which took place in two waves during the fifth and seventh years of prophethood. The Abyssinian emigration is significant in the biography of many Companions documented in Al-Isti'ab — it was the first time that the early Muslim community sought refuge from persecution, and the Negus (al-Najashi) of Abyssinia's protection of the Muslims is itself celebrated in Islamic tradition. Many of the Companions who emigrated to Abyssinia eventually returned to Arabia, either before or after the Hijra to Medina, and their double emigration earned them additional honor.

Among the most celebrated of the Muhajirun are the members of the Banu Hashim and Banu Makhzum clans, who provided many of the early converts. Khadija bint Khuwaylid (d. 3 BH / 620 CE), the Prophet's first wife and the first person to accept Islam, is treated in Al-Isti'ab's section on female Companions with great veneration. Though she died before the Hijra and is therefore not technically among the Muhajirun to Medina, her status as the first believer and the Prophet's most beloved wife gives her a unique position in the tradition. Ibn 'Abd al-Barr quotes the hadith in which the Prophet said: 'She believed in me when people disbelieved, she trusted me when people doubted, and she supported me with her wealth when people withheld theirs.'

Khadija and Abu Bakr together represent the two pillars of the earliest Islamic community — the first woman and first adult man to believe. Their entries in Al-Isti'ab set the standard for biographical treatment of the most distinguished Companions: comprehensive in documenting their family backgrounds, their relationship with the Prophet, their contributions to the faith, and their legacy in the scholarly and spiritual tradition.

Ibn 'Abd al-Barr pays particular attention to the question of priority (sabiqah) in accepting Islam — who was first, who was second, and how these early conversions are ranked. The scholarly tradition recognizes several distinct 'firsts': Khadija as the first person overall, Abu Bakr as the first free adult male, 'Ali ibn Abi Talib as the first child, Zayd ibn Haritha as the first freed slave, and Bilal ibn Rabah as the first of the oppressed class. These 'firsts' are not merely matters of historical trivia but carry theological significance, since the Quran itself honors those who were the earliest in faith: 'And the forerunners — the first of the Muhajirun and the Ansar — and those who followed them in goodness: Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him' (9:100).

The entries on the Muhajirun in Al-Isti'ab collectively paint a vivid picture of the social fabric of early Islam — the merchants, tribal leaders, former slaves, craftsmen, and young men and women who formed the first Muslim community. Their diversity of background and the unity of their faith provide a model of what the Islamic community can and should be: a community defined not by tribal loyalty or social class but by shared belief and mutual commitment to the divine guidance. This sociological dimension of Al-Isti'ab gives it value beyond the merely biographical, offering a window into the social revolution that Islam represented in seventh-century Arabia.
content_ar: المهاجرون الأوائل تركوا ديارهم وأموالهم ابتغاء رضوان الله، وقد أثنى الله عليهم في القرآن الكريم ثناءً عظيماً.
